items.text: After 10 PM, taxi fares may include a surcharge; Uber/Bolt surge pricing applies during peak nighttime hours. Plan accordingly or use night buses where available.

Polish roads have high accident rates; drivers are often aggressive and speed limits are strictly enforced by cameras. Wear seatbelts, avoid driving at night if unfamiliar, and never drink and drive (0.02% blood alcohol limit).

items.label: Documents and insurance
items.text: You need an international driving permit, passport, vehicle registration, and proof of third-party liability insurance. Police may fine you 500+ PLN for missing documents.

items.label: Vignette for highways
items.text: Motorways (A1, A2, A4) require a vignette (highway sticker) costing 10 PLN for a day or 100 PLN for a month. Buy at petrol stations or border crossings; traveling without one risks a 500 PLN fine.

items.label: Speed limits and enforcement
items.text: City limits are 50 km/h, regional roads 90 km/h, and motorways 130 km/h. Fixed and mobile speed cameras are widespread; fines start at 100 PLN and escalate quickly for high speeds.

items.label: Parking and clamping
items.text: Paid parking zones (marked with blue lines) are enforced strictly in city centers. Overstaying risks a 100+ PLN fine and wheel clamping; use paid apps like ParkU or Payu to manage time.

items.label: Fuel and service
items.text: Petrol stations (Shell, Orlen, BP) are common along main routes. Fuel prices fluctuate but are generally 1.5–2 PLN per liter; pay by card to avoid cash exchanges.

items.label: Warsaw Chopin (WAW) main hub
items.text: Poland's largest airport is 10 km south of central Warsaw. The S2 train (9 PLN, 30 min) connects to the city center; Uber/Bolt taxis cost 30–50 PLN and are faster but surge-priced during peaks.

items.label: Krakow Airport transfers
items.text: Krakow Airport (KRK) has a train station; the city center is a 15-minute ride (9 PLN). Alternatively, buses 208, 252, or 292 cost 4 PLN and reach the center in 45 minutes.
Airport taxi ranks are notorious for overcharging tourists. Always use the official pre-booked taxi counter, Uber, or Bolt instead of accepting offers from unofficial drivers.

items.label: EU travel requirements
items.text: EU/EEA citizens need a valid ID; non-EU citizens need a passport and may require a visa. Check entry requirements 3 months before departure.

items.label: Budget airlines and schedules
items.text: Ryanair, Wizz Air, and LOT Polish Airlines fly to/from Polish airports. Ryanair and Wizz Air often use secondary terminals; check your boarding pass for gate location.

items.label: Baltic coastal ferries
items.text: Polferries operates car and passenger ferries from Gdańsk and Świnoujście to Scandinavia (Sweden, Denmark). Book online; crossings take 10–15 hours and include cabin options.

items.label: River and lake cruises
items.text: Scenic boat trips operate on the Vistula River in Warsaw and Krakow, and on lakes in the Masuria region. Summer schedules are frequent; winter service may be limited.

items.label: International ferry documentation
items.text: For Scandi ferries, bring your passport and vehicle documents. Non-EU travelers should verify visa requirements for transit countries before booking.

items.label: City tourist cards
items.text: Warsaw's Warszawa Karta and Krakow's Kraków Card include unlimited public transport plus museum discounts. Cards for 24, 48, or 72 hours cost 46–80 PLN and 39–79 PLN respectively.

items.label: Monthly passes for residents
items.text: Living in a city? A monthly unlimited transit pass costs 100–150 PLN and covers all buses, trams, and metro. Register with a local address to buy at discount.

items.label: Digital vs. paper tickets
items.text: Most cities prefer digital tickets (via MKA, Jakdojade, or operator apps) to paper. Machines at stations and kiosks sell paper tickets but digital is often cheaper and always accepted.
Counterfeit or invalid tickets are sold by street vendors near tourist areas. Only buy from official ticket machines, kiosks, or verified apps to avoid 100+ PLN fines.

items.label: Student and senior discounts
items.text: Students and seniors over 65 qualify for 50% discounts on most transit passes with valid ID. Ask at kiosks or check the operator's website for eligibility.

items.label: Major carriers and prepaid plans
items.text: Orange, T-Mobile, and Plus dominate Polish mobile networks. Buy prepaid SIM cards at airport kiosks, supermarkets, or carrier stores; a starter pack with 10 GB costs 30–50 PLN.

items.label: Network coverage
items.text: 4G/LTE coverage is excellent in cities and along major highways. Rural areas may have slower 3G; check coverage maps on carriers' websites before traveling.

items.label: EU roaming rates
items.text: EU citizens can roam for free using their home plan at no extra cost. Non-EU travelers should buy a Polish SIM to avoid steep roaming charges; data is cheap locally (5–20 PLN per GB).

items.label: Wi-Fi availability
items.text: Free Wi-Fi is common in hotels, cafes, restaurants, and shopping centers. Download offline maps (Google Maps, Maps.me) before traveling to remote areas.
Public Wi-Fi in train stations and airports is sometimes slow or unreliable. Use your mobile data or a paid airport Wi-Fi pass (5–10 PLN) for critical tasks.
